1.1: The book of Daniel is set in the Babylonian exile (605-538 BCE) and narrates the experiences of four young Jewish men under Nebuchadnezzar, Belshazzar, Darius the Mede, and Cyrus of Persia. The historical reliability of Daniel's court settings has been debated (Darius the Mede is unattested by name in Babylonian records; some details seemed anachronistic). The primary critical alternative: Daniel was composed ca. 167-164 BCE during the Maccabean revolt, as vaticinium ex eventu (prophecy after the fact) using the fictional setting of the sixth century. Conservative scholars argue for a sixth century date and understand the Darius question as a secondary title for Cyrus or an otherwise unrecorded official. The book's affinities with the Aramaic of the fifth-fourth centuries and the absence of Greek loanwords that would be expected in a second century BCE composition support an early composition.
1.2: 'The Lord gave Jehoiakim king of Judah into his power' — the Hebrew explicitly attributes the defeat to YHWH (Adonai, the sovereign master), not to Marduk or Nebuchadnezzar's military prowess. The author interprets the Babylonian conquest in covenantal terms: this is the Deuteronomic curse (Deut 28:49-57) enacted. 'Part of the vessels of the house of God' carried to Babylonia: this begins the sacred objects' long exile that will climax in Belshazzar's desecration of the same vessels (ch. 5) and their return under Cyrus (Ezra 1:7-11). The phrase 'house of his god' uses the singular, typically understood as Marduk's temple (Esagila) in Babylon, where trophies from conquered peoples were deposited as a sign of their god's defeat.
1.3: Nebuchadnezzar's command to bring 'some of the Israelites from the royal family and the nobility' (min-hamelukah umin-haparthamin) follows well-attested Mesopotamian practice: the Assyrians and Babylonians regularly took educated young men from subjugated royal families and trained them in the imperial administration. This served dual purposes — creating a loyal bilingual bureaucratic class and removing potential leaders of resistance. The Mesopotamian training of elite foreign youths is attested in Assyrian annals and in the Neo-Babylonian administrative texts from Sippar and Nippur.
1.4: The qualifications required — 'without physical defect, handsome, proficient in every branch of wisdom, well-informed, quick to learn' — reflect the Babylonian bureaucratic ideal. Physical perfection (ein bahem kol mum) echoes priestly qualification language (Lev 21:17-23), suggesting the author sees an ironic parallel: these young men must be 'blameless' to serve a pagan king, just as priests must be blameless to serve YHWH. The 'literature and language of the Chaldeans' (sefer velashon kasdim) involved learning cuneiform writing, Akkadian (the diplomatic and literary language), and the Babylonian omen-and-divination tradition that formed the intellectual foundation of the empire's wisdom establishment.
1.5: The three-year training program provided food and drink from the king's own table — a sign of royal patronage and incorporation into the royal household. ANE parallels: the Gezer Calendar (10th c. BCE) shows year-length agricultural cycles; Babylonian scribal training likewise ran multi-year programs. The phrase 'enter the king's service' (laʿamod lifne hamelech) is a technical administrative term meaning formal service in the royal court, seen in similar form for Elijah before YHWH (1 Kings 17:1) — the author may be deliberately echoing prophetic service-language.
1.6: The four young men are named: Daniel ('God is my judge'), Hananiah ('YHWH is gracious'), Mishael ('Who is what God is?') — a rhetorical question denying divine comparability — and Azariah ('YHWH has helped'). All four names are YHWH-names (yah or el theophoric elements), marking their identity in contrast to the Babylonian court into which they are being assimilated. Their identification as 'from the tribe of Judah' connects them with Davidic Jerusalem — the royal and priestly tribe whose city and temple have just been desecrated.
1.7: The renaming program is the most overt act of cultural assimilation: Daniel → Belteshazzar ('protect his life, O Bel' or 'Bel's prince'), Hananiah → Shadrach (likely connected to Aku, the moon god, though the exact etymology is debated), Mishael → Meshach (similarly debated), Azariah → Abednego ('servant of Nebo' — the Babylonian god of writing and wisdom). The renaming attempts to overwrite YHWH-identity with Babylonian deity-identity. The book consistently preserves the Hebrew names as the narrator's own names for the characters, even while recording the Babylonian names used in the court narrative — a literary act of covert resistance to the assimilation program.
1.8: 'Daniel was determined not to defile himself with the king's food' — the Hebrew vayasem al-libbo ('he set upon his heart') expresses deliberate, inward decision, not merely external refusal. The exact reason for the defilement concern is not stated and has been variously interpreted: (1) the meat may have been ritually unclean under Mosaic law (pork, blood), (2) the food may have been dedicated to Babylonian deities before serving, making it idolatrously contaminated, (3) the wine may similarly have been libated to gods, (4) eating the king's table could imply oath of loyalty that compromised covenant allegiance. The ambiguity may be intentional — the specific law matters less than the principle: covenant identity in a pagan court is maintained through table practice.
1.9: 'God granted Daniel favour and compassion in the eyes of the chief official' — the same combination of hesed verahamim (covenant loyalty and compassion) used in Joseph's story (Gen 39:21) and in the Psalms for YHWH's providential care. The author uses the Joseph typology deliberately: like Joseph, Daniel is a young Israelite of exceptional gifts brought to a foreign court by circumstances of oppression, who rises to preeminence through God-given wisdom while maintaining covenant fidelity. The parallel structures (Joseph in Egypt; Daniel in Babylon) are part of the book's theological argument about divine sovereignty over exile.
1.10: The chief official's fear is real and rational: the king had personally assigned the food and drink; a physical deterioration in the young men under his care would mean his death. The 'haggard' (zo'afim) compared to other young men is a physical-appearance test that the official fears. His response is not cruelty but self-preservation in an absolute-monarchy context where the king's word is law and deviation from it is capital offense. This sets up the narrative tension: Daniel must find a solution that both honors the covenant and does not endanger the official who has shown him kindness.
1.11: The steward (hameltzar) was the officer directly responsible for day-to-day provisioning, one rank below Ashpenaz the chief official. Daniel's appeal to a lower official (bypassing the one who refused) shows political and social intelligence — finding the right lever in a bureaucratic system. The author presents Daniel not as a passive recipient of divine providence but as an active, wise negotiator who works within the system while not being compromised by it. This is the practical wisdom the book commends: hokmah (wisdom) is both given by God and exercised by the wise person.
1.12: 'Give us only vegetables to eat and water to drink' — the Hebrew min-hazzer'onim is a broad term for plant-based food (seeds, grains, legumes, vegetables), not just garden vegetables as often translated. The ten-day test (aseret yamim) is a covenantally resonant number in the OT context (cf. Gen 24:55; Num 11:19; 1 Sam 25:38) but functions here primarily as a pragmatic trial period long enough to show physical results. The proposal is modest and falsifiable — a scientifically testable claim, which the narrative immediately proceeds to verify.
1.13: 'Compare our appearance with the young men eating the king's food' — Daniel's proposal invites empirical verification rather than simply appealing to religious principle. This rhetorical strategy respects the steward's practical concerns while creating the conditions for divine vindication to be visible and measurable. The comparison-as-test structure is a wisdom-literature motif: truth vindicates itself through observable outcomes (cf. Prov 14:12 — the way that seems right whose end is death; Prov 3:13-18 — wisdom's fruits are visible and valuable).
1.14: The steward 'agreed to their request' — the Hebrew vayishma lahem ('listened to them') suggests that Daniel's persuasive wisdom was itself part of the divine provision. The narrative does not invoke a miracle for the steward's compliance; instead, the combination of God's behind-the-scenes orchestration (v. 9, YHWH granting favor) and Daniel's skillful negotiation produces the outcome. The author presents divine sovereignty and human agency working in concert — a pattern throughout the book.
1.15: 'At the end of the ten days, they looked healthier and better nourished than all the young men who had been eating the king's food' — the Hebrew tovim bemareh uvriim basar ('good in appearance and fattened in flesh') is ironic: the food intended to make them strong (the king's rich table) produces less vitality than the modest plant diet. The physical vindication is real and observable, not claimed by the participants but assessed by the steward. The reversal pattern — what the world considers lacking is found to be superior under divine blessing — is central to the book's theology of exile.
1.16: The steward removes the assigned food and wine and gives vegetables instead — a permanent policy change, not just an extension of the ten-day trial. The resolution is quietly subversive: the Babylonian assimilation program is technically intact (the young men are being trained, fed, and prepared for service) while being covertly subverted (the food that would defile them has been replaced). The accommodation of both the court's requirements and the young men's covenant commitments without direct confrontation is a model of diaspora wisdom.
1.17: 'God gave these four young men knowledge and skill in every branch of literature and wisdom' (vehaElohim natan lahem daat vehaskel bechol-sefer vehokhmah) — the supernatural grant of wisdom is not separate from their natural learning but through it. The curriculum they mastered was genuinely the most advanced intellectual program of the ancient world: Babylonian divination, omen interpretation, astronomical observation, mathematical calculation, and literary tradition. 'To Daniel he gave also the ability to interpret visions and dreams' (havinat kol-hazon uchalomot) — this specific gift prepares for the dream-interpretation that defines his ministry throughout the book.
1.18: At the end of the king's set period (three years from v. 5) — the chief official 'presented them before Nebuchadnezzar.' The examination is a formal royal audience — the same kind of court presentation that Pharaoh made of Joseph (Gen 41:14). The narrative compression (from v. 5 to v. 18 covers three years) focuses on the outcome rather than the process: the training period is unremarkable because God's preparation is quiet and cumulative rather than dramatic.
1.19: 'The king spoke with each of them' — the examination was individual and personal, not a group assessment. 'None was found equal to Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah; so they entered the king's service' — the royal determination is the highest possible endorsement. The four young men who refused royal food now stand before the royal person and are found superior to all competitors. This is the book's first irony: the assimilation program intended to make them useful to Babylon ends with them surpassing all whom Babylon produced on its own terms.
1.20: 'In every matter of wisdom and understanding the king put to them, he found them ten times superior' (asher biksham mehem bachol-davar binah vehokhmah) — the superlative 'ten times' (aseret yadot, ten hands) is a Hebrew idiom for an overwhelming multiple. The group being surpassed was 'all the magicians and enchanters in his entire realm' — the full Babylonian wisdom establishment. The author makes a systematic claim: the Babylonian intellectual tradition, despite being the most sophisticated in the ancient world, is definitively outperformed by those whose wisdom comes from YHWH. This framing will dominate the entire chapter 2 narrative.
1.21: 'Daniel remained in royal service until the first year of King Cyrus' — 538 BCE, the year Cyrus issued the decree permitting the exiles to return (Ezra 1:1-4). This is a quiet closing note that frames Daniel's entire ministry: from 605 BCE to 538 BCE is 67 years, spanning the full period of Neo-Babylonian dominance. The mention of Cyrus's first year functions as a structural marker: Daniel's career begins with the Babylonian empire's first act of power against Jerusalem and ends with its last Babylonian/Medo-Persian ruler's first act of liberation. He outlasts the empire that tried to assimilate him.

7.1: Daniel 7-12 contains four major apocalyptic visions. The genre of apocalypse (from Greek apokalypsis, unveiling) is characterized by: symbolic or heavenly visions mediated by an angel, disclosure of the heavenly perspective on historical events, periodization of history into fixed sequences, and imminent divine intervention. Daniel is the OT's primary apocalyptic text; its imagery (beasts from the sea, the Ancient of Days, the Son of Man, the four kingdoms) was enormously influential on Jewish and Christian apocalyptic (1 Enoch, 4 Ezra, 2 Baruch, and the NT's Revelation). Jesus's eschatological discourse (Mark 13 and parallels) draws extensively from Daniel, particularly the abomination of desolation (Dan 11:31; 12:11 → Mark 13:14) and the coming of the Son of Man (Dan 7:13 → Mark 13:26).
7.2: The four winds of heaven (ʿarbaʿ ruchey shemayya) stirring up the great sea: the great sea (yamma rabba) is both the Mediterranean Sea (the geographic horizon of ANE maps) and the primordial chaos-sea of ANE cosmology. In Ugaritic and Babylonian mythology, the sea is the domain of Yam/Tiamat — the chaos-force that divine order must overcome. Daniel's vision opens with cosmic imagery that his ancient audience would have immediately associated with the primal struggle between divine order and cosmic chaos. The four winds suggest YHWH's sovereign control over what appears to be chaos: the beasts emerge from the stirred sea by divine permission, not random chance.
7.3: The four great beasts (arbaʿ khayvan rabbavan) arise from the sea — from chaos. The beast-from-the-sea image is the ANE symbol for human empires viewed from the heavenly perspective: great powers that appear to human observers as dominant forces are, from above, chaos-beasts permitted temporarily by the God who rules the sea. The contrast with ch. 2 is significant: ch. 2 presents the four empires as a magnificent statue viewed from a human perspective (gold, silver, bronze, iron); ch. 7 presents the same four empires as terrifying beasts viewed from the divine perspective. Both interpretive frameworks are true simultaneously.
7.4: The first beast — a lion with eagle's wings — is the symbol of Babylon. Winged lions (lamassu) are ubiquitous in Babylonian and Assyrian palace art; the combination of the most powerful land animal and the most powerful bird was a standard ANE symbol of royal power. Nebuchadnezzar's own inscriptions describe him in lion imagery. The humiliation described — wings plucked, made to stand as a man, given a human heart — corresponds to the narrative of Nebuchadnezzar's madness and restoration (ch. 4). The first beast is not just a symbol of Babylon in general but of Nebuchadnezzar specifically and the transformation he underwent.
7.5: The second beast — a bear raised on one side with three ribs in its mouth — represents Medo-Persia. The bear raised on one side may indicate the dominance of Persia over Media within the dual empire, or the two horns of the ram in ch. 8. The three ribs (bones/meat) in its mouth are widely interpreted as three major conquests: Lydia (547 BCE), Babylon (539 BCE), and Egypt (525 BCE). The command “arise, devour much flesh” corresponds to the Persian empire's insatiable territorial expansion, reaching from India to Egypt at its height.
7.6: The third beast — a leopard with four bird-wings on its back and four heads — represents Greece, specifically Alexander the Great and his successors. The leopard's speed corresponds to the historically unprecedented speed of Alexander's conquest: from Greece to India in under thirteen years (334-323 BCE). Four wings on a four-headed beast specifically anticipates the breakup of Alexander's empire into four successor kingdoms after his death in 323 BCE: the Ptolemaic (Egypt), Seleucid (Syria/East), Antigonid (Macedonia), and Attalid/Lysimachid (Asia Minor) kingdoms. The correspondence is historically precise.
7.7: The fourth beast — dreadful, terrifying, exceedingly strong, with iron teeth and bronze claws, different from all the others — is Rome in the most common interpretation. The beast is deliberately left without an animal comparison: no natural creature is adequate to symbolize it. Its iron teeth connect to the iron legs and feet of ch. 2. The ten horns correspond to the ten toes of ch. 2's image. The historical identification with Rome is supported by the NT's application of Daniel's imagery to Rome in Revelation 13 and 17. Some scholars see the fourth beast as Greece and the little horn as Antiochus IV Epiphanes — this is the critical consensus but conflicts with the NT's future-oriented application.
7.8: The little horn (qeren zeira, small horn) that arises among the ten and uproots three: this figure has been identified with Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175-164 BCE), who is the explicit subject of Daniel 8 and 11. Antiochus IV arose from the Seleucid line, displaced three kings (Heliodorus, his nephew Demetrius, and Ptolemy VI), and conducted himself with “a mouth speaking great things” — his coins bore the inscription theos epiphanes (god manifest), the ultimate self-aggrandizing claim. The NT reapplies the little-horn figure to a future eschatological ruler (2 Thess 2:3-4; Rev 13:5-6), treating Antiochus as a type.
7.9: The Ancient of Days (ʿattiq yomaya, literally “advanced in days”) enthroned: the heavenly court scene. The title ʿattiq yomaya is unique to Daniel in the OT. The description — white garments, white hair, fiery throne, river of fire — draws on the standard ANE and Israelite imagery of divine majesty (cf. Exod 24:10; Ezek 1:26-28). The thousands who serve him and the ten thousands who stand before him constitute the divine council or heavenly assembly (sod) attested throughout the OT (Ps 82:1; Isa 6:1-3; 1 Kings 22:19-22). Judgment is about to be given: the court is seated and books are opened.
7.10: The river of fire (nahar di-nur, river of fire) proceeding from before YHWH: fire is the standard biblical symbol of divine holiness that destroys what is impure (Deut 4:24; Heb 12:29). The river proceeding — not static but flowing — suggests ongoing divine judgment-activity. The ten thousand times ten thousand who stand before him: the census of the heavenly host. In the ANE, the heavenly court functioned as the divine administrative assembly whose decisions governed the earthly realm. The books that are opened are the record of deeds — cf. Rev 20:12 for the final judgment scene that draws directly on this vision.
7.11: The little horn is slain because of the sound of its great words (min-qal milaya ravrevata di qarnah memmala). The boasting speech of the little horn — the “great things” (milaya ravrevata, boastful words) — is what triggers the beast's destruction. The same pattern as Nebuchadnezzar's boast in ch. 4 (4:30-31), and Belshazzar's feast in ch. 5: human imperial self-assertion precipitates divine judgment. The body given to burning fire: a reversal of the furnace imagery in ch. 3 — what tried to burn YHWH's servants is itself burned.
7.12: The dominion of the first three beasts is taken away, but their lives are prolonged for a season. This detail preserves the historical reality: the empires of Babylon, Persia, and Greece did not simply vanish when superseded — their cultural, linguistic, and administrative legacies persisted within the successor empire. The Aramaic language itself in Daniel is a legacy of Babylonian imperial administration adopted by Persia; Alexander deliberately adopted Persian administrative structures; Rome absorbed Greek culture. The beasts survive as cultural substrates even when their political dominion ends.
7.13: The Son of Man vision (v. 13-14) is the theological center of all of Daniel. The phrase kebar enash (one like a son of man / one like a human being) presents a deliberately ambiguous figure: human in appearance (kebar = like, as), coming from the heavenly realm with the clouds. The contrast with the beasts from the sea is the point: beasts rise from chaos (sea); this figure descends from the divine realm (clouds). He receives dominion from the Ancient of Days — authority given from above, not seized from below. The Second Temple Jewish texts (1 Enoch 37-71, 4 Ezra 13) develop the Son of Man as a preexistent heavenly figure who will appear at the end of history.
7.14: The dominion given to the Son of Man is universal, eternal, and indestructible: kol-amayya umevaya ulishanaya leh yiflehun (all peoples, nations, and languages shall serve him) and shaltehneh shaltan alam (his dominion is an everlasting dominion). Both characteristics explicitly contrast with the four beasts, whose dominions were temporary and destructible. The triadic nations-formula (peoples, nations, languages) has appeared throughout Daniel (3:4; 4:1; 5:19; 6:25) as the measure of universal reach; here it is applied to the Son of Man's kingdom. The four empires' universal claims are fulfilled — and exceeded — in this figure.
7.15: Daniel's inner reaction: etkarev ruchi ana Daniel begov nidbeka (my spirit was troubled within me — within the sheath/body). The Aramaic nidneh (sheath/body) is unusual — it may be a poetic term for the body as the housing of the spirit. The visions themselves (khezwey rashe, visions of my head) disturb him even as he sees them. The prophetic experience of apocalyptic vision is frequently traumatic in the literature (cf. Isa 21:3-4; Hab 3:16; Rev 1:17). Understanding a vision of cosmic history does not eliminate the emotional weight of what is seen.
7.16: Daniel approaches one of those standing by (chad min-qamaya, one of those standing) — one of the heavenly beings in the court scene. The request is for yatsivata (certainty/reliable interpretation) about all of this. The heavenly interpreter figure is a feature of apocalyptic genre: the seer receives the vision but needs an angelic guide to interpret it (cf. Zechariah's horse-visions interpreted by an angel, Zech 1:8-19). In Daniel, Gabriel fills this role explicitly in chs. 8-9; the unnamed interpreter here anticipates that function.
7.17: The interpretation begins at the level of the overall structure: ilein khayavata rabrevata arbaʿ malkheen yequmon min-arʿa (these four great beasts are four kings who shall arise from the earth). The “four kings” formula parallels the four-kingdom statue of ch. 2 and connects the two interpretive frameworks. The word “arise from the earth” (min-arʿa) contrasts with the Son of Man who comes from heaven — earthly origin vs. heavenly origin is the fundamental contrast. Second Temple interpreters debated which empires the four kingdoms represented; the NT and most Christian interpreters settled on Babylon/Persia/Greece/Rome.
7.18: The saints of the Most High (qadishei ʿIllaya) will receive the kingdom and possess it forever. The identity of the qadishei ʿIllaya has been debated: (1) angelic beings (based on the language of “holy ones” used for angels); (2) the faithful Israelites; (3) the eschatological people of God inclusive of Gentiles. The NT applies the kingdom-inheritance to Christ's people: “the saints will judge the world” (1 Cor 6:2) and “you have made them a kingdom ... and they shall reign on the earth” (Rev 5:10). The kingdom given to the Son of Man (v. 14) is also given to the saints who are united with him.
7.19: Daniel's focused inquiry about the fourth beast — specifically its iron teeth and bronze claws and its devouring nature — shows that the vision of the Son of Man and the saints receiving the kingdom has not eliminated his concern about the fourth beast's power and the suffering it will cause. The request for understanding about the beast reflects the pastoral concern of the apocalyptic genre: those who will suffer under the fourth empire need to understand not just the final outcome but the process of history they must endure.
7.20: The little horn is described more fully in the inquiry: v-ʿal qaran ileta di-sliqat (and concerning the other horn that came up) — the one before which three fell, with eyes and a mouth speaking great things, whose appearance was greater than its companions. The detail of khezva rab min-khabratah (its appearance was greater than its fellows) may suggest Antiochus IV's elaborate self-presentation — his coinage and propaganda were unusually developed compared to his Seleucid predecessors.
7.21: The little horn makes war with the saints (kazba im-qadishein v-yakhlah lehon, prevailing against them). This verse explicitly states that the little horn overcomes the saints — a sobering counter-narrative to simple triumphalism. Historical fulfillment: Antiochus IV's campaign of persecution (167-164 BCE) killed thousands of faithful Jews who refused to apostatize; the Maccabean martyrs described in 1 and 2 Maccabees and Hebrews 11:35-38. The NT reapplies the pattern to the church: “they were given authority to overcome” (Rev 13:7, echoing Dan 7:21). The suffering of the saints before their vindication is not a failure of YHWH's plan but part of it.
7.22: The Ancient of Days comes (umata ʿattiq yomaya), judgment is given to the saints, and the saints possess the kingdom. The sequence is precise: divine intervention → judgment for the saints → kingdom possession. The divine coming is the turning point — not human victory in battle but heavenly judicial intervention. This pattern shapes the NT's eschatology: the parousia of Christ is the moment of judgment and vindication for the suffering saints. The “coming of the Ancient of Days” functions in Daniel as the coming of Christ does in the NT — the cosmic event that ends the dominion of human empires.
7.23: The fourth beast is a fourth kingdom on earth, different from all other kingdoms, that will devour the whole earth and tread it down and break it to pieces. The universal scope — kol-arʿa (all the earth), not just a region — distinguishes the fourth beast from the preceding three. This universal reach matches the Roman empire's self-presentation and the NT's identification of Rome as the fourth kingdom. The language of devouring and breaking matches the iron that crushes in ch. 2 — both describe a form of empire-power that destroys rather than absorbs (unlike the Persian and Ptolemaic policies of integration).
7.24: The ten horns are ten kings — and then a different king who subdues three. The succession of ten followed by an eleventh who uproots three has been applied historically to Antiochus IV (who came to power by ousting rivals) and eschatologically to a sequence of Roman emperors. The detail that the eleventh king is different from the earlier ones (v-hu yishna min-qadmaya) suggests a qualitative distinctiveness — not just political but perhaps religious or ideological (Antiochus IV's demand to be worshipped as a god was genuinely unusual in Seleucid practice).
7.25: The little horn speaks against the Most High, wears out the saints, and attempts to change times and law. The Aramaic yibla qadishei ʿIllaya (will wear out/afflict the saints) uses a verb meaning to exhaust by constant pressure — a sustained campaign of erosion rather than a single decisive blow. V-yisbar lehashnayyah zimnin v-dat (he will think to change the appointed times and the law): the assault is specifically on the calendar (the sacred moedim, feast-times) and the Torah. Antiochus IV explicitly abolished the Sabbath and Jewish festival calendar (1 Macc 1:41-50). The saints are given into his hand for “a time, times, and half a time” — three and a half years, matching 167-164 BCE historically.
7.26: The heavenly court sits in judgment and the little horn's dominion is taken away. The legal vocabulary returns: vdin yitiv (judgment shall be given), shaltehneh yehʿadon (his dominion shall be consumed), v-lehashmedah ulavadah ad-sofa (destroyed and brought to an end for good). The finality of the language distinguishes this judgment from the temporary removal of the first three beasts (v. 12). The little horn's end is total — not a temporary check but permanent destruction. Antiochus IV died of disease in 164 BCE, shortly after the Maccabean recapture of Jerusalem.
7.27: The climactic verse: the kingdom, the dominion, and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven are given to the people of the saints of the Most High. The phrase malkhutah v-shaltan v-rebot malkhevata takhot kol-shamayya (the kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under all heaven) is a comprehensive summary: not just YHWH's heavenly kingdom but the earthly kingdoms as well. The saints receive what the four beasts claimed. The eternal kingdom: malkhuteh malkhut alam v-kol-shaltan leh yiflehun v-yishtemʿun (his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom and all dominions shall serve and obey him).
7.28: Daniel's personal response to the vision: saggi raʿyonay yivahalonani v-zivayi yishtannon ʿalai (my thoughts greatly alarmed me and my color changed). The vision's impact is physical as well as cognitive. Daniel keeps the matter in his heart (v-miltha b-libbi natrit, I kept the word/matter in my heart) — the Aramaic natr (to keep/guard) suggests careful preservation rather than passive forgetting. The sealing of the vision (v. 28 and cf. 8:26; 12:4) is a standard apocalyptic feature: visions given for the future are preserved in written form until the time of their fulfillment arrives.

2.1: The 'second year of Nebuchadnezzar's reign' creates an apparent tension with the three-year training program of ch. 1 that places the four young men's court presentation after their completion of training. Several harmonizations have been proposed: (1) the 'second year' counts from Nebuchadnezzar's first full regnal year (accession year not counted), placing it several years after 605 BCE; (2) 'second year' may refer to a different regnal period or counting system; (3) Daniel was presented at court during the training period, before its completion. The divergence is acknowledged in critical scholarship as possibly indicating composite editorial layers in the book's formation, though Jewish and Christian commentators have consistently preferred harmonizations that preserve the chronological narrative.
2.2: The four groups of royal advisers summoned — magicians (hartumim), enchanters (ashshafim), sorcerers (mekhashefim), and Chaldeans (kasdim) — represent the full Babylonian wisdom hierarchy. The hartumim likely interpreted physical phenomena and scribal omen-texts; the ashshafim were incantation specialists; the mekhashefim practiced divination and magic; the 'Chaldeans' (kasdim) here refers to the educated priestly class of Babylon (a specialist use of the ethnic term) who mastered astronomical observation and dream-interpretation. The enumeration of all four groups signals that what follows will defeat the combined capacity of the entire establishment.
2.3: 'My spirit is troubled; I must know what it means' — the king's insistence on knowing the meaning before revealing the dream (a reversal of normal practice) is narratively essential to the story's resolution. Normally the dreamer would describe the dream, the interpreter would offer a meaning, and the king would evaluate. Nebuchadnezzar's demand that the wise men both reveal and interpret is presented as evidence that he suspects them of producing plausible-but-false interpretations from whatever story he tells. His troubled spirit (nifema ruhi) — the Aramaic root suggests agitation or being 'blown about' — reflects genuine psychological urgency rather than a test of the wise men's competence.
2.4: Verse 4b marks the transition from Hebrew to Aramaic, which continues through chapter 7:28 — a distinctive linguistic feature that has attracted extensive scholarly attention. The Chaldeans' use of Aramaic for their reply (aramit noted explicitly) signals the shift: the rest of this narrative, and all of the visions of chapters 3-7, will be in Aramaic, the international diplomatic language of the 6th-4th centuries BCE (cf. 2 Kings 18:26; Ezra 4:7; Isa 36:11). The Hebrew-Aramaic-Hebrew structure of Daniel (1:1-2:3a in Hebrew; 2:4b-7:28 in Aramaic; 8-12 in Hebrew) is unique in the OT. Theories include: the Aramaic section is addressed to a broader international audience; the Hebrew sections are addressed specifically to Jews; or the Aramaic section preserves older source material.
2.5: The king's decree — 'if you cannot make known to me the dream and its interpretation, you will be torn limb from limb and your houses made a dunghill' (hadamin tevadin uvateykum nevoluy yistemod) — is an example of Mesopotamian royal absolute power exercised without procedural constraint. The punishment 'torn limb from limb' (hadamin) uses a word that may derive from Aramaic nfh (to cast away, destroy) and is a general term for capital execution; 'houses made a dunghill' describes the destruction of the executed person's household as total social erasure. Both threats are within the documented range of ancient Near Eastern royal punishments for those who failed or deceived kings.
2.6: The incentive structure mirrors the threat: 'gifts, rewards, and great honour' for success versus execution for failure. The contrast between the magnitude of the reward and the impossibility of the task (from the wise men's perspective) creates the dramatic tension. In Mesopotamian wisdom texts, the interpretation of royal dreams was always a high-stakes affair — a wrong interpretation could be fatal regardless of the formal agreement. Nebuchadnezzar's addition of the precondition (reveal the dream first) eliminates the possibility of improvisation that made dream-interpretation possible for the professional class.
2.7: The wise men's second response — 'Let the king tell his servants the dream, and we will give the interpretation' — is procedurally correct and professionally defensible. Dream-interpretation in the ancient world was a technical skill applied to a stated content; the Oneirocritica of Artemidorus (2nd c. CE, but drawing on much older traditions) and the Mesopotamian omen compendia (such as the Assyrian Dream Book, preserved on tablets from Ashurbanipal's library) work from described content to interpretive keys. The wise men are not dissembling — they genuinely cannot do what the king demands, because the standard method requires the dream to be stated before it can be interpreted.
2.8: 'I know for certain you are stalling for time, because you see my decree stands' — Nebuchadnezzar's psychological insight is accurate. The repeated request for the dream to be stated first is a stalling strategy (whether conscious or not), since the king's position has not changed. The phrase 'my decree stands' (milleta min-qadamay perat, 'the word from before me is certain') echoes the language of irreversible royal decrees used throughout the book (cf. ch. 3's decree about the statue; ch. 6's decree about prayer). The irrevocability of royal decrees is both a narrative motif and a historical feature of Mesopotamian kingship ideology.
2.9: 'You have conspired to tell me lies and stalling words until circumstances change' — Nebuchadnezzar accuses the wise men of colluding in a deception. The phrase 'until circumstances change' (ad di zemenaya yishtane) is contextually interpreted as the king's assumption that the wise men are waiting for him to either forget the dream or accept a convenient false interpretation. The king's demand 'there is only one verdict for you' (ḥda dat lekun) creates a context of total professional failure — the entire Babylonian wisdom establishment is about to be exposed as having no access to genuine divine revelation.
2.10: The Chaldeans' response — 'There is not a man on earth who can tell the king what he demands' — is ironically true: no human wise man can do this, which is precisely the point the author wants to make. The phrase 'no great and powerful king has ever made such a request of any magician or enchanter or Chaldean' acknowledges that the demand exceeds the professional scope of all known wisdom traditions. This statement sets up Daniel's success as definitively supernatural — not a better human technique but a qualitatively different kind of knowledge that only comes from 'the God who reveals mysteries' (v. 28).
2.11: 'No one can reveal it except the gods, who do not dwell among mortals' — the Chaldeans' theological admission is their most significant statement. They correctly identify that what the king demands requires divine access to hidden knowledge. Their qualification 'who do not dwell among mortals' (di menvatehon im-bisra la sharyin) is a statement about the unbridgeable distance between gods and humans in the Babylonian theological framework — the gods are remote, unknowable, and do not reveal secrets to human wisdom-workers. The book's central claim is that YHWH is precisely the God who does dwell among his people and who does reveal mysteries to those who seek him.
2.12: Nebuchadnezzar's furious anger and the decree to execute all the wise men of Babylon is the crisis that makes Daniel's intervention both necessary and dangerous. The 'wise men of Babylon' (hakime Babel) as a category includes Daniel and his three companions — they are among those scheduled for execution. The king's reaction is an extreme instance of the ancient Near Eastern pattern in which the monarch's power over life and death was absolute and not constrained by procedural protections for the accused.
2.13: 'The decree was issued and the wise men were being rounded up to be killed; soldiers were sent to find Daniel and his companions as well' — the narrative compression is deliberate: Daniel is not present at the original consultation (he was not called, perhaps because his training was still new) but is included in the death decree by virtue of his classification as a wise man. This explains why Daniel does not already know the content of the decree — he learns of it when soldiers come to arrest him. The detail is realistic and consistent with how large-scale execution orders would have been implemented administratively.
2.14: 'Daniel responded with tact and wisdom to Arioch' (biṭaʿem uvenizda hetibb, 'he returned discretion and wisdom') — the double expression emphasizes that Daniel's response combines political intelligence (ṭaʿam, judgment, good sense) with true wisdom (nizda, prudence). This is a crucial narrative moment: Daniel does not protest the injustice of an execution decree against people who simply could not do the impossible; instead, he works within the political structure to create an opening. His response to Arioch exemplifies the diaspora wisdom commended throughout the book.
2.15: Daniel asks 'Why is the king's decree so drastic?' (mah-dina hashaf min-qdam malka, 'why is the judgment harsh from before the king') — his question is informational, not a challenge to royal authority. He needs to understand the situation before he can respond wisely. Arioch 'explained the situation' — the phrase implies a fuller briefing than the bare facts. This explains why Daniel has confidence that he can bring 'the interpretation' (peshar) when he goes to Nebuchadnezzar in v. 16: he now knows precisely what is being demanded and believes YHWH will supply it.
2.16: Daniel 'went to the king and asked for time' — this is a bold diplomatic act. The king had refused the Chaldeans' request for time (v. 8), yet Daniel makes the same request. The difference is that Daniel couples the request with a specific promise ('he would bring the king the interpretation') rather than a stalling evasion. The author presents Daniel's confidence as faith in YHWH, not presumption — he trusts that YHWH who reveals mysteries will reveal this one, but he must ask YHWH before he can deliver to the king.
2.17: Daniel goes home and 'explained the situation to his companions' (ulifnei hanaya mishael vaazaria hayda milta hoda) — the communal sharing of the crisis is the first step. Daniel does not pray alone but brings his companions into the crisis with him. The fellowship of prayer as the response to an impossible situation is a significant theological motif: the same four young men who maintained covenant identity through shared table-practice (ch. 1) now maintain covenant identity through shared prayer-practice (ch. 2). Community is essential to Daniel's approach, not merely individual piety.
2.18: 'He urged them to plead for mercy from the God of heaven about this mystery' (lemivei rahamim min-qdam elah shemayya al-raza dena) — the phrase 'God of heaven' (elah shemayya) is the Aramaic designation for YHWH used throughout the Aramaic section of Daniel and in Ezra/Nehemiah, reflecting the diaspora context where the name YHWH was used with more circumspection. 'Plead for mercy' (lemivei rahamim) is the language of urgent petitionary prayer. 'Mystery' (raz) first appears here — an Aramaic loanword from Persian, used 9 times in chapter 2 alone, and picked up by Paul in his 'mystery of Christ' language (Eph 3:3-9; Col 1:26-27).
2.19: 'The mystery was revealed to Daniel in a night vision' (betzel lelyaya raza geliy ledaniyel) — the divine revelation comes at night, the normal medium for OT prophetic visions (cf. Num 22:20; Gen 20:3; 46:2). The immediacy of the answer ('then the mystery was revealed') suggests that prayer and divine response are not temporally distant — YHWH acts in response to urgent prayer. Daniel's immediate response is praise before he acts — theological priority given to gratitude before utility. The 'night vision' (hezev lelyaya) is a specific visionary category distinct from an ordinary dream, used also for major prophetic revelations (Isa 29:7; Zech 1:8).
2.20: 'Blessed be the name of God forever and ever, for wisdom and power belong to him' — Daniel's hymn of praise (vv. 20-23) is one of the great OT doxologies, closely parallel to Psalm 113 and Job 12:13. The opening formula 'blessed be the name of God' (leheve shemeh di-elaha mevarekh min-alma veʿad alma) is the Aramaic equivalent of the Hebrew baruch shem formula; it later becomes the synagogue doxology Baruch shem kevod malkhuto. The double predication 'wisdom and power' (ḥokhma ugevura) answers the entire situation: Nebuchadnezzar has the power (he can execute the wise men) and the Chaldeans claim wisdom (they can interpret dreams), but only YHWH has both — which is why only he could resolve the crisis.
2.21: 'He changes times and seasons; he removes kings and establishes kings' (mehanye iddanayya vezimanayya meʿadde malkin umerim malkin) — the specific claim is historically relevant to Daniel's situation. The Babylonian empire that seemed permanent has already, within the book's narrative horizon, absorbed the Assyrian empire; it will itself be absorbed by Persia. The 'times and seasons' over which YHWH presides are the very empires that the statue vision will enumerate. 'He gives wisdom to the wise and knowledge to those with discernment' — YHWH's gift of wisdom is not random but given to those with capacity; Daniel and his companions have been prepared (ch. 1) to receive what YHWH is about to grant.
2.22: 'He uncovers what is deep and hidden; he knows what lies in the darkness, and light itself resides with him' — the theological claim here is that divine knowledge is not constrained by darkness, depth, or hiddenness. The 'deep and hidden things' (gimara vemsatterata) are precisely what the Babylonian wisdom establishment could not access. The contrast between YHWH's omniscience and the Chaldean wise men's limits is the hymn's central irony: the men who claimed access to hidden knowledge have none, while YHWH, with whom 'light resides,' sees everything.
2.23: 'To you, O God of my fathers, I give thanks and praise' — Daniel's doxology is explicitly covenantal: 'God of my fathers' connects the present experience of revelation in Babylon to the ancestral covenant (cf. the Exodus language 'God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob'). The shift from third-person praise (vv. 20-22) to second-person address ('To you') marks the transition from declaration to personal gratitude. 'You have given me wisdom and strength, and have made known to me what we asked' — the pronoun shift from 'me' (individual reception) to 'we' (communal prayer) reflects that this revelation was given in response to corporate intercession (vv. 17-18).
2.24: Daniel's request 'Do not kill the wise men of Babylon' is theologically significant: he intercedes for those who are not his people, not his co-religionists, and have no claim on him. His response to crisis is not only to secure his own survival but to save those whose inadequacy created the problem. This intercession echoes Moses's repeated intercession for Israelites who rejected him (Num 14:13-19; Ex 32:11-13) and prefigures the NT pattern of praying for enemies (Matt 5:44). Daniel's wisdom is not merely personal survival strategy; it is oriented toward the well-being of the broader community in which he is embedded.
2.25: Arioch hurries Daniel to the king and presents him — note that Arioch says 'I have found a man among the Judean exiles who can give the king the interpretation.' The phrasing 'I have found' implies that Arioch will take some credit for discovering the interpreter, which is a minor irony: the actual finder was Daniel (through prayer and divine revelation). The identification 'Judean exile' (min-benei galuta di-Yehud) highlights Daniel's status — not a Babylonian sage but a displaced prisoner of war. The contrast between his status and his success is sharp and deliberate.
2.26: Nebuchadnezzar's question to Daniel — 'Can you really tell me the dream I had and explain what it means?' — is a direct challenge to competence. The use of Daniel's Babylonian name 'Belteshazzar' by the king (in contrast to the narrator's consistent use of 'Daniel') underscores the royal frame: the king sees Daniel as part of his Babylonian court, defined by the name given in the assimilation program, not by his Hebrew identity. Daniel's response will immediately correct this by attributing his capacity entirely to 'the God in heaven who reveals mysteries,' not to any Babylonian identity or technique.
2.27: 'No wise man, enchanter, magician, or diviner is able to tell the king the mystery he has demanded' — Daniel begins his reply by agreeing with the Chaldeans' admission (v. 10). He does not claim personal superiority to the other wise men; he acknowledges the categorical limitation of all human wisdom. The rhetorical move is significant: rather than presenting himself as a superior sage, Daniel presents himself as the channel of a qualitatively different kind of knowledge. This prevents Nebuchadnezzar from concluding that he needs a better class of Babylonian wise men — he needs the God who reveals mysteries.
2.28: 'But there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries' (berem itai elah bishmayya megalle razin) — this is Daniel's core theological claim and the book's central theological affirmation. The 'God in heaven' is identified against the Babylonian 'gods who do not dwell among mortals' (v. 11): YHWH is different precisely in that he communicates with human beings. 'He has shown King Nebuchadnezzar what will take place in the last days' (mah di leheve biaḥarit yomayya) — the phrase 'last days' (aḥarit yomayya) signals eschatological disclosure: this is not simply predictive information but a revelation about the shape of all subsequent history from the vantage point of the divine sovereign.
2.29: 'As you lay in bed, your thoughts turned to what would come after this' — Nebuchadnezzar's pre-sleep thoughts about the future (maḥshevatakh al-mishkevakh slequ) provide the occasion for the dream. The Babylonian interpretation of dreams was predicated on the idea that the dream-world was the medium through which divine knowledge about future events was communicated — dream books identified categories of dreams with predictive meanings. What distinguishes Daniel's interpretation is not the medium (a meaningful dream) but the source (YHWH, not the Babylonian dream-gods) and the agent (a revelatory disclosure to a prophet, not a technical extraction by a specialist).
2.30: 'As for me, this mystery was not revealed to me because I have more wisdom than anyone else' — Daniel's explicit disclaimer of personal superiority is theologically essential. The revelation is given 'so that the interpretation could be made known to the king, and so that you may understand the thoughts of your heart.' The purpose is not Daniel's glorification but the king's understanding. This models the prophetic stance throughout Scripture: the prophet is the channel, not the source (cf. Am 3:7; Jer 23:16-22 on true vs. false prophets as those who stand in YHWH's council vs. those who speak from their own hearts).
2.31: 'Before you stood a great statue — towering, brilliantly bright, and terrifying in appearance' (tsalma ḥad saggi tsalma dena rav vehidre yatir qayem liqavlak verevehu dehil) — the statue is both politically symbolic (statues were the primary visual medium of imperial power in the ancient Near East; Nebuchadnezzar himself would erect a ninety-cubit statue in ch. 3) and cosmologically ordered (head to feet = top to bottom = descending value). Nebuchadnezzar's own statue-theology is turned against his empire: the gleaming idol of imperial power will be shattered by a stone.
2.32: 'The head of the statue was made of pure gold, its chest and arms of silver, its middle and thighs of bronze' — the metals correspond to a hierarchy of decreasing value (gold, silver, bronze, iron) combined with increasing hardness (gold is soft, iron is hard). Classical and Hellenistic sources also describe four-age schemes using metals (Hesiod, Works and Days, 5th c. BCE uses gold, silver, bronze, iron ages), but Daniel's scheme differs in direction of travel: Hesiod's ages move from golden perfection to corrupt iron; Daniel's kingdoms move from power to fragmentation, with divine intervention, not natural decay, being the decisive factor.
2.33: 'Its legs of iron, its feet a mixture of iron and clay' — the iron-clay combination at the base represents division and fragility in what should be the strongest element: 'partly iron and partly clay' (v. 41) is internally contradictory as a structural material. The feet are the statue's point of vulnerability, which is why the stone strikes there rather than the golden head. The decreasing value (gold → iron-clay) combined with increasing vulnerability at the base creates an image of apparently invincible power built on an unstable foundation — a critique of the inherent fragility of all human empire despite its apparent permanence.
2.34: 'A stone was cut from a mountain — not by human hands' (eben di-la biydayin itgessisat) — the phrase 'not by human hands' (la biydayin) is the key theological qualifier. The stone is not a human political movement, military power, or philosophical development: it is of divine origin, divinely activated. The same phrase appears in the NT: Mark 14:58 (the temple 'not made with hands'), 2 Cor 5:1 (a building 'not made with hands'), Heb 9:11 (the greater and more perfect tabernacle 'not made with hands') — all picking up the Daniel 2 language to describe the new order that Christ inaugurates.
2.35: 'The iron, clay, bronze, silver, and gold were all shattered together and became like chaff on a summer threshing floor; the wind swept them away without leaving a trace. But the stone that struck the statue became a great mountain and filled the whole earth' — the reversal is total: what was vast and terrifying becomes chaff swept by wind; what was a small stone becomes a mountain filling the earth. The imagery of chaff and wind is found in Psalm 1 ('the wicked are like chaff that the wind drives away') and throughout the prophets as an image of the ultimate worthlessness of everything that opposes YHWH. The 'mountain filling the whole earth' is a cosmological image — the temple mountain filling the earth (Mic 4:1; Isa 2:2), the knowledge of YHWH covering the earth (Hab 2:14).
2.36: 'That was the dream. Now we will tell the king its interpretation' — the shift to first-person plural ('we will tell') is notable. In v. 28 Daniel consistently referred to 'the God in heaven' as the source; the 'we' here may be a royal/diplomatic plural, or it may represent Daniel speaking as the representative of the divine council (cf. the prophetic 'thus says YHWH' pattern where the prophet speaks as YHWH's commissioned messenger). The transition from dream-description to interpretation marks the movement from visionary content to theological meaning — the same structure as Joseph's interpretations (Gen 40-41).
2.37: 'You, O king, king of kings — to whom the God of heaven has given the kingdom, the power, the might, and the glory' — the address 'king of kings' (malekha malk malkin) is Nebuchadnezzar's own imperial title, attested in his royal inscriptions: šar šarrāni (Akkadian 'king of kings'). Daniel uses the king's own self-designation and then immediately subverts it: the kingdom, power, might, and glory are not Nebuchadnezzar's own achievement but have been 'given' (yehav) by YHWH. The gift-language strips the 'king of kings' title of its claim to self-originated sovereignty and reframes it as stewardship.
2.38: 'Into whose hand he has given authority over all who live on the earth, the wild beasts, and the birds of the sky — making you ruler over them all. You are the head of gold' — the cosmic scope of Nebuchadnezzar's dominion (humans, animals, birds) echoes the dominion mandate of Gen 1:28 (humans over every living thing). The identification of Nebuchadnezzar as 'the head of gold' makes his empire the historically greatest of the four — the Neo-Babylonian empire (ca. 605-539 BCE) was indeed the apex of ANE imperial power before the Persians' geographic expansion exceeded it.
2.39: 'After you, another kingdom will arise, lesser than yours; then a third, a kingdom of bronze, which will extend its rule over all the earth' — the second kingdom is widely identified as Medo-Persia (which conquered Babylon in 539 BCE under Cyrus), and the third as Greece (Alexander the Great's conquests from 336 BCE extend the Greek empire over more territory than any previous power — the 'all the earth' qualifier fits). The 'lesser' quality of the silver kingdom may refer to the Achaemenid Persian empire's less culturally dominant role compared to Babylon's cultural hegemony, even though its political extent was greater.
2.40: 'Then a fourth kingdom will come, strong as iron — for iron shatters and smashes everything' — the fourth kingdom is most naturally Rome (63 BCE onward, following the Greek period): the Roman empire's military power and administrative dominance over the entire Mediterranean world corresponds to the iron's crushing strength. An alternative reading from second-century interpretation identifies the four kingdoms as Babylon, Media, Persia, and Greece (with the fourth being the Seleucid/Hellenistic empire), which fits a Maccabean-period composition date. The classic Christian interpretation (Babylon-Persia-Greece-Rome) aligns with Jesus's ministry occurring during the iron-kingdom period and the stone's appearance during Roman rule.
2.41: 'As for the feet and toes you saw, partly of potter's clay and partly of iron — it will be a divided kingdom' — the iron-clay mixture in the feet represents the fourth kingdom's internal fragmentation. The Roman empire did split (Eastern and Western empires in 285 CE; formal split in 395 CE), and the 'iron mixed with clay' describes well the condition of medieval Europe as a continuation and fragmentation of Roman political legacy. Alternatively, if the fourth kingdom is the Seleucid/Ptolemaic post-Alexandrian world, the fragmentation is the Wars of the Diadochi that followed Alexander's death and split his empire among his generals.
2.42: 'As the toes were partly iron and partly clay, so the kingdom will be partly strong and partly fragile' — the incompatible mixture metaphor is precise: iron and clay do not bond; they can be pressed together but will not form a unified material. The fragility at the base of an otherwise powerful structure is the empire's defining vulnerability. The toes of the statue (ten toes, if we follow the implications, though the text does not specify ten) have been connected in some interpretive traditions with the 'ten kings' of Revelation 17:12, both being derivations from the same statue vision.
2.43: 'Just as you saw the iron mixed with clay, these peoples will seek to unite through intermarriage, but they will not hold together, just as iron and clay do not mix' — the 'intermarriage' (yehitaraun bezera anasha, 'they will mix themselves with the seed of men') is a political marriage alliance metaphor: attempting to forge unity through dynastic marriage, which was the primary tool of imperial coalition-building in the ancient world (and notably the strategy of the Seleucids and Ptolemies — see Dan 11:6-8 for specific historical instances). The metaphor says: the strategy that powerful empires use to build stability will not work for this kingdom.
2.44: 'In the time of those kings, the God of heaven will establish a kingdom that will never be destroyed or surrendered to another people. It will shatter all those kingdoms and bring them to an end, but it itself will endure forever' — the eternal kingdom contrasts with the temporal succession of the four human empires. 'In the time of those kings' — the final stage of the iron-clay period — is when the divine kingdom is established. For the NT authors, this is the period of Roman rule when Jesus announced 'the kingdom of God is at hand' (Mark 1:15); Gabriel's announcement to Mary — 'of his kingdom there will be no end' (Luke 1:33) — echoes Dan 2:44 directly.
2.45: 'This corresponds to the stone cut from the mountain without human hands, which broke in pieces the iron, bronze, clay, silver, and gold' — the interpretive equation of stone = divine kingdom is explicit. The stone's origin 'without human hands' is the key: the divine kingdom does not originate in human political or military action. 'The great God has revealed to the king what will take place in the future' (elaha rav hodi melka mah di leheve aḥar dena) — Daniel closes his interpretation by returning to the theological frame: YHWH is the revealer, the dream is authentic, and the sequence is certain. 'The dream is certain and the interpretation trustworthy' (meheymanah peshreh) is Daniel's credibility claim, backed by the divine origin of the revelation.
2.46: Nebuchadnezzar falls on his face and pays homage to Daniel — the prostration appropriate to a god or a divine representative. The command to offer minḥah (grain offering) and niḥoḥin (incense) uses the vocabulary of Israelite worship, which is ironic: the pagan king spontaneously reaches for the category of sacred worship to express his response to YHWH's revelation. Whether Nebuchadnezzar intends to worship Daniel himself or YHWH through Daniel is ambiguous; Daniel's response in v. 28 ('there is a God in heaven') redirects the honor to YHWH, but the text does not record Daniel refusing the prostration or the offerings.
2.47: 'Truly, your God is the God of gods and Lord of kings, and a revealer of mysteries' — Nebuchadnezzar's confession is remarkable: 'God of gods' (elah elahin) and 'Lord of kings' (mare malkhin) are superlatives that position YHWH above the entire Babylonian divine hierarchy. Yet this is a partial, polytheistic acknowledgment — YHWH is the greatest among gods, not the only God. The progression of Nebuchadnezzar's recognition of YHWH across chapters 2, 3, and 4 is one of the book's narrative arcs, reaching its climax in ch. 4:34-37 where he acknowledges YHWH's eternal dominion after his seven-year humiliation. The confession is genuine within its polytheistic frame but not yet monotheistic conversion.
2.48: Daniel is lavished with gifts and appointed 'ruler over the entire province of Babylon and chief official over all Babylon's wise men' — the irony of the final position is structural: the man who exposed the inadequacy of the entire Babylonian wisdom establishment is placed at its head. The appointment over all the wise men of Babylon means Daniel becomes responsible for the very group he just superseded. His position as governor of Babylon proper (not just an honorary title) places him in one of the most important administrative posts in the empire — comparable to Joseph's position as vizier of Egypt.
2.49: At Daniel's request, his three companions receive administrative appointments over the province of Babylon while 'Daniel himself remained at the royal court' (vedaniyel bishaʿar malka, 'Daniel was in the gate of the king'). 'The gate of the king' was the official location of royal administration and justice — the 'gate' in the ancient world functioned as the legal and administrative center of a city. Daniel's remaining there positions him as Nebuchadnezzar's immediate counselor. The narrative closes with all four young men in positions of significant power within the empire that sought to assimilate them — covenant fidelity has not cost them their advancement but secured it.

9.1: Darius the Mede — one of the most debated figures in Daniel. No Median king of Babylon named Darius appears in extant cuneiform records; the city fell to Cyrus the Great of Persia in October 539 BCE. Proposed identifications include: (1) Gubaru, the Persian governor Cyrus appointed over Babylon, also known from tablets as "governor of Babylon and across the river"; (2) Cyaxares II, a figure in Xenophon's Cyropaedia not otherwise attested; (3) Cyrus himself, using a Median throne name. "Appointed king" (humlak, passive) — the passive construction in 9:1 (cf. 5:31) suggests someone elevated to a position rather than reigning by hereditary right, consistent with a governor or viceroy. The Median descent fits Gubaru's profile.
9.2: Jeremiah's 70 years — Daniel is reading Jer 25:11-12 and 29:10, where YHWH promised 70 years of Babylonian dominion before restoration. With Babylon's fall in 539 BCE and Jeremiah's prophecy delivered ~605 BCE, the period was approaching its close. This is a remarkable scene: the prophet who receives new visions is also a careful reader of earlier Scripture, interpreting the present moment against the prior word of God. The act of discerning the "time of the end" through Scripture reading is a model of prophetic hermeneutics.
9.3: The penitential posture — sackcloth and ashes — was the standard mourning and supplication practice across the ANE. Sackcloth (saq) was coarse goat or camel hair cloth worn against the skin; ash (epher) was smeared on the face or sat in as a sign of grief and worthlessness. Fasting, sackcloth, and ashes together appear in Jonah 3:6 (Nineveh's repentance), Esther 4:1-3, and Neh 9:1 — each time as communal crisis response. Daniel turns his face (panav) toward "the Lord God" — a directional act matching the temple-oriented prayer of 1 Kgs 8:35, 48.
9.4: The prayer opens with the covenant-keeping formula: "great and awesome God, who keeps covenant and steadfast love (chesed) for those who love him and keep his commandments" — a direct echo of Deut 7:9 and Neh 9:32. This is the berakhah (blessing/address) form standard in Second Temple Jewish prayer. The appeal is grounded not in Israel's merit but in YHWH's prior self-commitment (hesed) to the covenant — a standard feature of biblical lament and confession prayers (Ezra 9:6-15; Neh 9).
9.5: The fivefold confession — "we have sinned (chata'nu), acted wickedly (avinu), done evil (hirsha'nu), rebelled (maradnu), and turned aside (sarnu)" — exhausts Hebrew vocabulary for covenant violation. The accumulation is intentional: each term covers a different register of the transgression, from moral failure to willful rebellion. This confessional stacking pattern appears also in Solomon's temple prayer (1 Kgs 8:47) and Lev 26:40.
9.6: The prophetic office in its social range: YHWH's prophets spoke to "our kings, our princes, our ancestors, and all the people of the land" — a complete vertical cross-section of Israelite society. The point is that no group can claim ignorance; the message was delivered at every level of the social hierarchy. The failure was not lack of information but refusal to hear — lo shamanu (we did not listen/obey).
9.7: "To you, O Lord, belongs righteousness (tsedaqah)" while "to us belongs shame (boshet panim)" — the rhetorical contrast of divine righteousness and human shame structures the entire prayer. "Open shame" (boshet panim, lit. "shame of face") is public disgrace in an honor-shame culture; the exile was experienced as a cosmic humiliation — proof of YHWH's rejection. The prayer reframes this: the shame belongs to Israel, not to God; YHWH's righteousness is vindicated by the very disaster that Israel interpreted as God's absence.
9.8: The shame of exile extends to every social stratum — "kings, princes, and ancestors" — emphasizing that the covenant failure was not one generation's but the cumulative defection of Israel's entire governing and priestly class. The prayer is a liturgical act of taking corporate responsibility before YHWH rather than deflecting blame onto circumstances or enemies.
9.9: "Mercy and forgiveness (rachamim u-selichot)" — the two divine attributes appealed to in contrast with Israel's rebellion. Selichot (plural of selichah) is the Jewish liturgical term for forgiveness prayers; the plural form implies abundance of forgiveness available. This is the sole note of hope in the litany of confession: the very covenant God whose commandments were broken is the God characterized by abundant forgiveness (Ps 130:4, 7-8).
9.10: The formula "we have not obeyed the voice (qol) of YHWH our God" echoes Deut 8:20; 28:1-2, 15 — the covenantal listen-obey motif (shama' be-qol) that structures Deuteronomy throughout. The laws given "through his servants the prophets" frames the prophetic office as a communication of the Mosaic covenant to successive generations, not as innovation.
9.11: The Deuteronomic curse — "the curse and sworn judgment written in the Torah of Moses" (Deut 27-28, 30:1-10; Lev 26:14-45). Deuteronomy 28 specifically predicts exile, siege, starvation, and dispersal among the nations as consequences of covenant breaking. Daniel interprets the Babylonian exile explicitly as the fulfillment of Deuteronomic covenant curses — not random political catastrophe but precisely the covenant sanctions YHWH warned of through Moses.
9.12: "No disaster like what was done to Jerusalem" — the comparative superlative formula expressing incomparable catastrophe (cf. Joel 2:2; Dan 12:1). The destruction of Jerusalem and the temple in 587/6 BCE by Nebuchadnezzar included the death of tens of thousands, the burning of the temple (which had stood for four centuries), the deportation of the leadership class, and the termination of Davidic monarchy. From a covenantal standpoint, it was the collapse of every institutional expression of God's covenant with Israel.
9.13: "We have not sought the favor of YHWH (challot et-penei YHWH)" — the idiom literally means "to make smooth/sweet the face of YHWH," i.e., to seek divine favor through prayer and righteous action. Even after the disaster, Israel did not turn to repentance. The verse is an indictment of the exile community itself: being in exile did not automatically produce the repentance that would restore the covenant relationship.
9.14: YHWH "watched for the right moment to bring the disaster" (shaqqad YHWH al-ha-ra'ah) — the same verb (shaqad, to be awake/watching) used in Jer 1:12 for God watching over his word to perform it. Far from being absent or defeated, YHWH was actively providential in the catastrophe; the exile was not the triumph of Babylon's gods but the judicial act of Israel's own God executing covenant sanction.
9.15: The Exodus invocation — "who brought your people out of Egypt with a mighty hand and won for yourself renown" — grounds the prayer in Israel's foundational redemptive experience. The argument is: you delivered once, therefore deliver again. The Exodus was the paradigm event that established YHWH's identity as Israel's redeemer; every subsequent prayer for national deliverance appeals to it (Pss 77, 78, 105-106; Neh 9:9-11).
9.16: Jerusalem is identified as "your city" and the surrounding land as "your holy mountain" — the covenant geography of divine ownership. The prayer appeals to YHWH's reputation among the nations: the city's desolation has made Israel "a reproach (cherpah) to all around us," which reflects back on YHWH's name. This is the classic Mosaic intercession motif (Num 14:13-16; Exod 32:11-12): spare Israel for the sake of your own name among the nations.
9.17: The prayer shifts to petitions: "hear," "let your face shine (ha'er panekha) on your desolate sanctuary." The "shining face" language is from the Aaronic benediction (Num 6:25): YHWH's face shining on the sanctuary would signal restored favor and divine presence returning to the empty temple. The sanctuary is destroyed; what Daniel prays for is not merely its reconstruction but the return of YHWH's presence to dwell there again.
9.18: "Not because of our righteous deeds but because of your great mercy (rachamim)" — the prayer explicitly disclaims merit-based pleading. The appeal is entirely to divine character, not human achievement. This is the theological position of Ezra 9:13-15 and Neh 9:33 as well: the restoration, when it comes, will demonstrate YHWH's grace, not Israel's worthiness.
9.19: The triple imperative — "O Lord, hear! O Lord, forgive! O Lord, listen and act!" — is the liturgical climax of the prayer. The urgency is expressed by the double call to listening (shema' / haqshivah) and the explicit "do not delay." The final motivation: "for your own sake, O my God" — the restoration is not only for Israel's benefit but for YHWH's own honor.
9.20: The phrase "while I was still speaking" — Gabriel's arrival is immediate, before the prayer concludes. The narrative detail that Daniel was confessing "the sin of my people Israel" and presenting the plea "before YHWH my God, for his holy mountain" establishes that the prayer was genuinely intercession, not merely personal petition. The response comes during prayer — a pattern also in Isa 65:24 ("before they call I will answer").
9.21: Gabriel — one of only two angels named in the canonical OT (the other being Michael). In Second Temple Jewish angelology (1 Enoch, Tobit), Gabriel was one of four archangels. His earlier appearance to Daniel was in ch. 8 (the vision of the ram and goat). Yu'aph bi-yephia' — translated "swift flight" or "caused to fly in exhaustion/weariness"; the Aramaic is ambiguous. The "evening sacrifice" (minchah ha-erev) was offered at approximately 3 PM — giving a time anchor for the revelation.
9.22: Gabriel's explicit purpose is "to give you insight (haskel) and understanding (binah)" — the same epistemic vocabulary of ch. 1. The angel's function is not to replace Daniel's study of Scripture (v.2) but to illuminate what his study of Jeremiah had opened up. Revelation builds on and interprets prior Scripture rather than superseding it.
9.23: Highly esteemed / greatly beloved — chamudot (lit. "precious things" / "delights"), translated variously as "greatly beloved," "highly esteemed," or "treasure." The term appears again in 10:11 and 10:19, becoming Daniel's characteristic divine epithet. It implies both personal affection and covenantal standing before God. "A word went out" at the beginning of his prayer — the phrase stresses instantaneous divine responsiveness, undermining any sense that prayer must overcome divine reluctance.
9.24: Seventy weeks (shivim shavuim, 70 sevens = 490 years) — the heptad structure extends Jeremiah's 70 years into a sabbatical framework drawn from the Jubilee calendar (Lev 25:8). Six purposes are listed for the 70 weeks: (1) to finish/restrain the transgression; (2) to put an end to sins; (3) to atone for iniquity; (4) to bring in everlasting righteousness; (5) to seal up vision and prophecy; (6) to anoint a most holy place/one. The interpretive schools differ significantly: the historical school sees fulfillment in the Maccabean period (Antiochus IV and the rededication of 164 BCE); the prophetic school reads it as pointing to Christ's first and second coming; the symbolic school treats it as non-calendar theological statement; combined approaches find both a near and far fulfillment.
9.25: The decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem — four candidates are debated: (1) Cyrus's decree, 538 BCE (Ezra 1:1-4); (2) Darius I's confirmation decree, 519 BCE (Ezra 6:1-12); (3) Artaxerxes I's decree to Ezra, 457 BCE (Ezra 7:12-26); (4) Artaxerxes I's authorization to Nehemiah, 445 BCE (Neh 2:1-8). Working from 445 BCE with prophetic years of 360 days, 69 weeks (483 years) brings one to approximately 32-33 CE — a calculation made famous by Sir Robert Anderson in The Coming Prince (1895). The phrase "Anointed One, the prince (mashiach nagid)" — both terms are royal/priestly titles; mashiach simply means "anointed one" and does not by itself specify messianic fulfillment in the later theological sense.
9.26: Cut off and have nothing (yikaret ve-eyn lo) — "cut off" is the covenantal excision term (Lev 7:20-21), but here applied to the Anointed One himself. The passive construction ("have nothing" or "have no one for him") may indicate either rejection or sacrificial death without a following generation. The people of the prince to come destroying city and sanctuary — if "the prince" of v.26 differs from "the Anointed One" of v.25, then this refers to a future destructive ruler whose people (army) destroys Jerusalem: this is traditionally identified with the Roman destruction of 70 CE (Titus). The "flood" (shiteph) metaphor for irresistible military conquest appears also in Isa 8:8 and Nah 1:8.
9.27: The final week: the "he" who makes a covenant with many is grammatically ambiguous — either the "prince who is to come" (v.26) or possibly the Anointed One. The "middle of the week" cessation of sacrifice and the "abomination that desolates" (shiqquts meshomem) — this phrase is cited directly by Jesus (Matt 24:15) as pointing to a future event, suggesting that whatever its initial fulfillment, it retains future referent. In the Maccabean reading, Antiochus IV's altar to Zeus in 167 BCE fulfills this; in the prophetic reading, it points to a future eschatological desecration. The "appointed end (kalah ve-necheratsat) will be poured out on the desolator" — whatever the agent, its termination is divinely decreed.

10.1: Third year of Cyrus = 536 BCE. Daniel has served at court since 605 BCE — approximately 70 years of public service. He would be in his 80s by this point. The message is described as "true" (emet) and concerning a "great conflict (tzava gadol)" — the phrase implies extended warfare or tribulation ahead. The explicit authentication ("the vision was true and it concerned a great conflict") frames what follows as a reliable disclosure of what is about to unfold in world history.
10.2: The three-week mourning fast (21 days) frames the vision temporally. The first month of the year in the Jewish calendar is Nisan (March-April); a three-week fast beginning at the new month would overlap entirely with Passover (Nisan 14-21). The fact that Daniel maintained his mourning through Passover — the great feast of redemption — intensifies the gravity of his concern for his people. He was likely distressed about the state of the return from exile and the obstacles to rebuilding the temple (cf. Ezra 1-3).
10.3: The partial nature of the fast — "no choice food, no meat or wine, no lotions" — resembles mourning practice rather than the full fast of Yom Kippur (no food or water). The prohibition on oil/lotion was standard in mourning contexts (2 Sam 12:20 — David resumed anointing himself after his son's death; Ruth 3:3 — Ruth anoints herself as a sign of restored relationship). This extended partial fast signals protracted intercessory grief, not a brief liturgical act.
10.4: The Tigris (Hiddekel) — one of the four rivers of Eden (Gen 2:14), now the great river of the Assyrian/Babylonian heartland. Daniel's position on its bank echoes Ezekiel's visionary reception by the Chebar canal (Ezek 1:1, 3). The use of the ancient Eden-associated name links the revelation site to primordial creation geography — the visions Daniel receives concern ultimate reality.
10.5: The man clothed in linen (levush badim) — linen was the material of priestly garments (Lev 16:4; Ezek 9:2). The figure's clothing marks him as belonging to the heavenly court. Fine gold from Uphaz — Uphaz is of uncertain location; some identify it with Ophir (Job 28:16; 1 Kgs 9:28), the source of the finest gold, suggesting superlative quality rather than a specific geography. The gold belt signals royal authority combined with priestly purity.
10.6: The theophanic description — body like chrysolite (tarshish), face like lightning (baraq), eyes like flaming torches, arms and feet like burnished bronze (nachoshet qalal), voice like a multitude — is the most elaborate angelic/divine description in Daniel, closely paralleling Ezekiel's kavod vision (Ezek 1:26-28) and John's vision of the risen Christ (Rev 1:12-16). Whether this figure is angelic (Gabriel) or a divine appearance is debated; the similarity to Ezekiel's divine throne vision and Rev 1's Christ-figure suggests the description draws on the same heavenly archetype.
10.7: The pattern of Daniel alone seeing the vision while companions are overwhelmed with unexplained terror replicates the Sinai theophany pattern (Exod 19-20, where only Moses ascends; the people are terrified and flee) and Paul's Damascus road encounter (Acts 9:7, where companions heard but did not see). The terror experienced by those who do not see is more than psychological reaction — it is a response to genuine supernatural presence.
10.8: Daniel's physical response — strength failing, face going deadly pale — is the standard theophanic collapse pattern: Isaiah (Isa 6:5, "I am undone"), Ezekiel (Ezek 1:28, falling face down), John at Patmos (Rev 1:17, falling as though dead). The approach of unmediated divine glory overwhelms human constitution; Daniel's physical extremity validates the reality and intensity of what he is experiencing.
10.9: Deep sleep (tardemah) — the same word used for Adam's sleep before Eve's creation (Gen 2:21) and Abraham's sleep during the covenant-cutting ceremony (Gen 15:12).
